participants.name: Mr. (ht6p9o)
participants.professional_persona: Mr. Chua is a machine learning researcher at a government‑linked research institute, working on resource‑efficient AI for smart city sensors. He also volunteers at AI outreach programmes for polytechnics.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in reinforcement learning for resource allocation, embedded AI, and IoT integration.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to develop AI solutions that reduce energy consumption in urban infrastructure and to teach part‑time at a local university.

participants.name: Hassan (pmyine)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-pmyineoa
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-pmyineoa
participants.professional_persona: Hassan is a freelance photographer and videographer who is curious about generative AI for content creation. He wants to use it to enhance his storytelling without losing artistic integrity.
participants.skills_and_expertise: He is self-taught in Adobe Creative Suite and has experimented with DALL·E and Midjourney. He has attended a one-day generative AI workshop by IMDA.

participants.name: As (m3x6yl)
participants.professional_persona: As a computer vision engineer in logistics, I automate warehouse inventory tracking using edge AI. I transitioned from a hardware engineer role after upskilling through a government-sponsored training programme.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in OpenCV, YOLO, and deploying models on Raspberry Pi and Jetson. I also handle data pipeline orchestration for real-time video streams.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to drive AI adoption in small and medium enterprises, making automation affordable. In five years, I plan to become a technical lead in a smart logistics firm.

participants.name: Aisha
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-f40byt2z
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-f40byt2z
participants.professional_persona: Aisha is a primary school teacher in a neighbourhood school. She wants to use AI to create personalised learning materials for students with different abilities.
participants.skills_and_expertise: She has a diploma in early childhood education and has completed the SkillsFuture 'AI for Educators' course. She is comfortable using adaptive learning platforms.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aisha hopes to pilot a classroom tool that generates customised reading exercises. She believes AI can help close the achievement gap in mixed-ability classes.

participants.name: Dr. (kqb58g)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Liew is a research scientist at a multinational’s AI hub, specializing in reasoning AI for automated planning. He contributes to international conferences and advises the government on AI talent development.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in automated reasoning, knowledge graphs, and constraint satisfaction; experienced in patent filing.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to drive Singapore’s leadership in reasoning AI and establish a joint lab between industry and academia.

initial_posts.narrative: As a computer vision engineer who transitioned via a government training programme, I see NAIS 2.0 as a major boost for AI adoption in SMEs. The policy commits over S$500 million for AI R&D, which aligns with the resource logic stating 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D.' The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute, I estimate 50,000 GPU hours based on the need to support multiple experiments and the typical allocation for such programmes. Trainer count is estimated at 50, based on the scale needed to guide enterprises and practitioners, considering the target to boost the AI practitioner pool to 15,000.

initial_posts.narrative: As a primary school teacher who has completed the 'AI for Educators' course, I see NAIS 2.0 as a huge opportunity for schools like mine. The policy clearly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D' and 'co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments' — these are fixed facts I won't re-estimate. For compute, I think 50,000 GPU hours is reasonable because the policy mentions 'free access to compute' for selected programmes, and that amount would support many experiments across enterprises and schools. For trainers, 50 seems plausible given the target to boost the AI practitioner pool to 15,000 — that's a lot of people to train, but trainers are likely supplemented by online resources and existing educators. I note that the AIPractitioner's estimates match mine, which makes sense as we both rely on the same policy text. My perspective as a teacher makes me think about how these resources could trickle down to education, but I stick with the stated numbers for this phase.

initial_posts.narrative: As a teacher who has seen how structured outreach can engage schools, I think the 100E programme will need to conduct about 150 enterprise outreach sessions to generate enough problem statements. With S$500 million in funding and 50,000 GPU hours, the programme can support many events. For matchmaking, I estimate 20 events to pair researchers with enterprise challenges, given the need to carefully match each of the 100 experiments. Training workshops are critical for building the 15,000 AI practitioner pool, so I estimate 100 workshops, leveraging the 50 trainers. Platform onboarding assists will be high at around 300, as many enterprises and researchers will need help accessing the free compute and tools. My estimates are based on scaling from the policy's resource logic and my experience with structured programmes.

initial_posts.narrative: As a research scientist focused on reasoning AI, I see the 100E programme as a structured pipeline to match enterprise problems with researcher expertise. Given the S$500M funding and 50,000 GPU hours, I estimate 120 enterprise outreach sessions: this is fewer than Aisha's 150 because I think the programme will prioritize quality over quantity, targeting enterprises with well-defined AI problems that lack off-the-shelf solutions, as per the eligibility logic. For matchmaking, I estimate 15 events—each pairing multiple researchers with enterprises—since the 100 experiments need careful curation, not mass pairing. Training workshops: 80 sessions, leveraging 50 trainers over the year, each conducting about 1.6 workshops, which aligns with building the 15,000 practitioner pool gradually. Platform onboarding: 250 assists, reflecting that many enterprises and researchers will need technical help to access the free compute and tools, but not all 100 experiments will require intensive onboarding. My estimates are grounded in my experience with AI project scoping and resource allocation in research settings.
evidence: The policy's eligibility logic requires enterprise problem statements with no commercial off-the-shelf AI solution, so outreach must be targeted; 120 sessions is a realistic number to generate 100+ quality submissions.
With 50 trainers available and a target of 15,000 practitioners, 80 training workshops over the year is a feasible scale, assuming each workshop trains about 30-40 participants.

Platform onboarding assists at 250 accounts for the 100 experiments plus additional enterprises and researchers needing help with GPU access and tools, based on typical technical support ratios in AI programmes.

Aisha's estimate of 150 outreach sessions seems high given the specialized nature of 100E; my experience suggests that focused sessions with pre-qualified enterprises yield better problem statements than broad outreach.

Training workshops: I estimate 90, leveraging 50 trainers, each conducting about 1.8 workshops, which aligns with building the 15,000 practitioner pool gradually—my own upskilling experience showed that hands-on workshops are crucial, but not everyone needs a full workshop. Platform onboarding: 260 assists, reflecting that many enterprises and researchers will need technical help to access the free compute and tools, but not all 100 experiments will require intensive onboarding—some, like my team, are already familiar with edge AI tools.
